---
title: Development of human innate immune responses in a humanized mouse model expressing four human myelopoiesis transgenes
author: "[FILL: Author names, affiliations]"
date: today
date-format: 'YYYY-MM-DD'
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    reference-location: section
keywords: [frontiers_immunology, research]
---

# Abstract

Background: Dysregulated innate immune signaling drives inflammatory pathology across a spectrum of human diseases, yet the absence of tractable in vivo systems for studying human-specific immune responses remains a central obstacle to translational research. Humanized mouse models engrafted with human hematopoietic cells offer a path toward bridging this gap, but most platforms support only limited myeloid reconstitution due to species incompatibility in key myeloid growth factor signaling.

Methods: NSG-QUAD mice, which transgenically express human IL-3, GM-CSF, SCF, and CSF1, were sublethally irradiated and reconstituted with human CD34+ hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs). At 5–6 weeks post-reconstitution, mice were subjected to intraperitoneal (IP) or intranasal (IN) lipopolysaccharide challenge, and innate immune responses were assessed by flow cytometry and multiplex cytokine measurement. Pharmacological validation was performed using the NLRP3 inhibitor MCC950.

Results: By 6 weeks, human myeloid cells were robustly engrafted across peripheral blood (~18% monocytes, ~12% dendritic cells, ~9% granulocytes of human CD45+ cells), spleen (~65% human CD45+), and liver (~55% human CD45+), with all three classical, intermediate, and non-classical monocyte subsets present. IP LPS elicited strong NF-κB-dependent cytokine production (serum TNF ~50 ng/ml, IL-6 and IL-8 similarly elevated), type I interferon pathway activation (serum CXCL10 ~90 pg/ml, 9-fold increase), and NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated release of human IL-1β (12-fold) and IL-18. IN LPS produced comparable compartment-restricted responses in lung and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid. MCC950 selectively suppressed IL-1β and IL-18 without altering TNF, IL-6, or monocyte survival.

Conclusion: Humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount functional human NF-κB, type I interferon, and NLRP3 inflammasome responses and are amenable to pharmacological modulation. The selective abrogation of inflammasome cytokines by MCC950 in the absence of monocyte cytotoxicity indicates that in vivo NLRP3-dependent IL-1β and IL-18 release occurs independently of LPS-induced monocyte death, providing a mechanistic insight with direct implications for inflammasome-targeted therapy development.

# Introduction

Innate immune cells are the first responders to infection and tissue injury, detecting conserved molecular patterns through dedicated receptor families and initiating downstream signaling cascades that shape both local and systemic inflammation. Among the best-characterized of these cascades are NF-κB-dependent transcription, which drives pro-inflammatory cytokine gene expression; type I interferon signaling, which coordinates antiviral and immunomodulatory programs; and NLRP3 inflammasome activation, which mediates post-translational processing of IL-1β and IL-18 (Swanson et al., 2019). Although each pathway is individually essential for protective immunity, their dysregulation underlies severe autoinflammatory syndromes, sepsis, chronic inflammatory bowel disease, and a growing list of complex multifactorial disorders (Dinarello, 2018).

Despite extensive mechanistic work in conventional mouse models, translating preclinical innate immunity research into effective human therapies has proven difficult. The innate immune system exhibits striking species differences: human and murine toll-like receptor repertoires, cytokine networks, and inflammasome compositions diverge at multiple levels, meaning that conclusions drawn from mouse models do not always predict human biology or clinical drug responses (Seok et al., 2013). Studying human innate immune pathways in controlled in vivo settings therefore requires dedicated experimental systems that cannot be replicated using standard inbred strains.

Humanized mouse models, generated by engrafting human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs) into immunodeficient hosts, provide one solution. NOD-scid-Il2rγ−/− (NSG) mice support efficient HSPC engraftment and have enabled substantial advances in human adaptive immunology; however, the absence of appropriate cross-reactive growth factor signaling in standard NSG mice results in poor development of human monocytes, dendritic cells, and granulocytes—the key effectors of innate immunity (Shultz et al., 2012). This myeloid deficiency severely limits the utility of conventional NSG-based platforms for innate immunity research.

Targeted transgenic expression of human myeloid growth factors can rescue this limitation. NSG-SGM3 mice, expressing human SCF, GM-CSF, and IL-3, markedly improve granulocyte and monocyte engraftment, while NSG-CSF1 mice, expressing human CSF1, selectively enhance macrophage and monocyte development (Wunderlich et al., 2010; Rios et al., 2019). NSG-QUAD mice combine all four transgenes—IL-3, GM-CSF, SCF, and CSF1—creating a host environment that simultaneously supports multiple branches of human myelopoiesis. Whether this enhanced myeloid compartment translates into functional innate immune competence in vivo has not been systematically established.

Here we report that humanized NSG-QUAD mice reconstituted with human HSPCs develop a diversified human myeloid compartment across blood, spleen, and liver by 6 weeks post-engraftment, and mount coordinated NF-κB, type I interferon, and NLRP3 inflammasome responses to both systemic and mucosal LPS challenge. Using the selective NLRP3 inhibitor MCC950, we demonstrate pathway-specific pharmacological modulation, establishing humanized NSG-QUAD mice as a tractable in vivo platform for studying human innate immunity and evaluating therapeutics targeting these pathways.

# Methods

Mice and humanization

NSG-QUAD mice (NOD.Cg-Prkdcscid Il2rgtm1Wjl Tg(CMV-IL3,CSF2,KITLG)1Eav Tg(SIRPA)1Flv/MloySzJ, JAX stock #028657) express human IL-3, GM-CSF, SCF, and CSF1 transgenes on a NOD-scid IL2Rγnull background to support human myeloid cell development [unverified]. [FILL: age and sex of mice at time of HSPC injection] were housed under [FILL: animal housing conditions, light cycle, diet]. Mice received [FILL: exact irradiation protocol — source, dose rate, timing relative to HSPC injection] 100–150 cGy irradiation followed by intravenous injection of 50,000–100,000 human CD34+ hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells isolated from cord blood. Human CD45+ engraftment was assessed by flow cytometry at weeks 5 and 6 post-reconstitution; mice with >10% hCD45+ cells in peripheral blood were included in subsequent experiments. All animal procedures were approved under ethics protocol EC2022-003 (VIB-UGent Center for Inflammation Research), and cord blood use was approved under protocol BC-06143.

Human HSPC isolation and characterization

Human CD34+ HSPCs were isolated from cord blood obtained from [FILL: number of cord blood donors used across all experiments] donors under [FILL: informed consent procedures for cord blood collection]. CD34+ cells were enriched using [FILL: HSPC isolation method — magnetic bead selection kit, vendor, catalog number]. HSPC purity was verified by [FILL: HSPC purity assessment method and typical purity achieved], and viability was assessed by [FILL: HSPC viability assessment before injection] before injection.

LPS challenge models

Lipopolysaccharide from Escherichia coli O111:B4 (Sigma-Aldrich L2630) was prepared in [FILL: LPS preparation — endotoxin unit verification, storage conditions, vehicle]. For systemic inflammation, LPS was administered intraperitoneally at 15 μg per mouse in [FILL: PBS vehicle control volume and route for each challenge model]. For mucosal inflammation, LPS was administered intranasally at 6 mg/kg body weight in [FILL: intranasal LPS administration method — anesthesia, volume, technique]. Control mice received equivalent volumes of PBS vehicle by the same routes. Mice were sacrificed 6 hours post-challenge for sample collection.

MCC950 treatment

MCC950 (MedChemExpress HY-12815A), a selective NLRP3 inflammasome inhibitor (Li et al., 2022), was prepared in [FILL: MCC950 formulation — solvent, final concentration, preparation protocol]. MCC950 was administered intraperitoneally at 50 mg/kg body weight 1 hour before LPS challenge. Vehicle control mice received the same volume of [FILL: vehicle composition for control group] by the same route and timing.

Sample collection

Mice were euthanized under [FILL: terminal anesthesia method and dose]. Blood was collected by cardiac puncture and allowed to clot at room temperature for 30 minutes before centrifugation to obtain serum. Bronchoalveolar lavage was performed via tracheal cannulation with four sequential 1 ml aliquots of PBS; recovered fluid was pooled and centrifuged to separate cells from BAL fluid supernatant. Spleen and liver were harvested and divided for flow cytometry and cytokine analysis. For flow cytometry, tissues were processed by [FILL: tissue processing protocol for flow cytometry — digestion enzymes, incubation times, cell isolation method]. For cytokine measurements, tissue samples were homogenized in [FILL: tissue homogenization method for cytokine measurement — buffer composition, homogenizer type, protein extraction protocol], and homogenates were clarified by centrifugation before analysis.

Flow cytometry

Single-cell suspensions from peripheral blood, spleen, and liver were stained with [FILL: complete antibody panel — clone, fluorochrome, vendor, catalog number for each antibody] antibodies against human CD45, CD3, CD19, CD14, CD16, HLA-DR, CD11c, CD66b, and CD15 to identify immune cell subsets. Monocyte subsets were classified as classical (CD14++CD16−), intermediate (CD14++CD16+), and non-classical (CD14+CD16++) based on CD14 and CD16 expression. Cell viability was assessed using [FILL: live/dead stain identity, vendor, catalog number]. Samples were acquired on a BD LSRFortessa flow cytometer, collecting [FILL: number of events acquired per sample] events per sample. Gating strategies included [FILL: gating strategy details — FSC/SSC gates, doublet exclusion, compensation controls]. Data were analyzed using FlowJo software version 10.

Cytokine measurements

Human cytokines (TNF, IL-6, IL-8, IL-1β, IL-18, IFNα2, and CXCL10) were quantified in serum, BAL fluid, and tissue homogenates using [FILL: Luminex kit vendor, catalog number, panel composition] Luminex multiplex immunoassay. Samples were diluted [FILL: sample dilution factors for each matrix] before analysis. Tissue cytokine concentrations were normalized to total protein content, which was determined by [FILL: protein quantification method for tissue normalization — assay type, vendor], and expressed as pg cytokine per mg total protein. The lower limits of detection were [FILL: lower limit of detection for each cytokine], and assay precision was characterized by [FILL: intra-assay and inter-assay coefficients of variation].

Statistical analysis

Data were log-transformed to satisfy normality assumptions and analyzed by two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Sidak's multiple comparison test using [FILL: statistical software used for analysis]. [FILL: assumptions testing for ANOVA — normality, homogeneity of variance] were verified before analysis. [FILL: criteria for outlier exclusion, if any] were applied. Statistical significance was defined as p < 0.05. Sample sizes (n = 6–9 mice per group) were determined based on preliminary experiments and are indicated in individual figure legends. Data are presented as mean ± SEM.

# Results

NSG-QUAD mice support robust human myeloid cell engraftment

To establish whether NSG-QUAD mice reconstituted with human hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs) could support functional human innate immune responses, we first characterized the kinetics and composition of human immune cell engraftment across peripheral blood, spleen, and liver compartments (Figure 1A). At 5 weeks post-HSPC injection, peripheral blood contained approximately 42% human CD45+ cells among total leukocytes (n=8), with B cells representing the dominant population at ~65% of human immune cells (n=7), minimal T cells (~0.2%, n=7), and monocytes comprising ~18% of human immune cells (n=7) (Figure 1B). By 6 weeks, human CD45+ cells constituted ~35% of peripheral blood leukocytes (n=8), with a diversified myeloid compartment including monocytes (~18%, n=7), dendritic cells (~12%, n=7), granulocytes (~9%, n=6), and neutrophils (~1.0%, n=7) (Figure 1C). 

Spleen and liver tissues showed even more pronounced human myeloid cell engraftment at 6 weeks post-reconstitution. Splenic tissue contained ~65% human CD45+ cells among total leukocytes (n=9), with monocytes (~20%, n=7), granulocytes (~21%, n=8), and dendritic cells (~4%, n=8) representing the major myeloid populations (Figure 1D). Liver tissue harbored ~55% human CD45+ cells (n=8), including granulocytes (~8%, n=8), monocytes (~3%, n=8), and dendritic cells (~3%, n=6) (Figure 1E). The higher proportion of granulocytes in spleen and liver compared to peripheral blood suggests preferential tissue localization of these cells, consistent with their roles in tissue surveillance and inflammatory responses (Crespo et al., 2017).

Detailed phenotypic analysis of peripheral blood monocytes revealed the presence of all three major human monocyte subsets (Kapellos et al., 2019): classical monocytes (CD14++CD16-) comprised ~47% of total monocytes (n=9), intermediate monocytes (CD14++CD16+) represented ~20% (n=9), and non-classical monocytes (CD14+CD16++) accounted for ~10% (n=7) (Figure 1F). The presence of all three subsets indicates that NSG-QUAD mice support not only quantitative engraftment but also qualitative differentiation of human monocytes into functionally distinct populations. Only mice achieving >10% human CD45+ engraftment in peripheral blood were included in subsequent inflammatory challenge experiments (n=6-9 per group), ensuring sufficient human immune cell representation for robust cytokine responses.

Humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount NF-κB-dependent cytokine responses to LPS

Having established robust human myeloid cell engraftment, we next examined whether these cells could mount canonical inflammatory responses to lipopolysaccharide (LPS), a prototypical activator of the NF-κB pathway (Fu et al., 2021). Intraperitoneal administration of LPS induced robust production of human tumor necrosis factor (TNF), interleukin-6 (IL-6), and interleukin-8 (IL-8) in serum, with TNF reaching ~50 ng/ml (25-50× over PBS control ~1-2 ng/ml; p<0.001, n~6-7 per group), IL-6 ~8 ng/ml (~40× increase; p<0.001), and IL-8 ~10 ng/ml (~10× increase; p<0.001) at 6 hours post-challenge (Figure 2A). These cytokines are canonical NF-κB target genes (Nam, 2006), and their coordinate induction demonstrates that human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice respond to systemic LPS with functional NF-κB pathway activation.

Liver tissue showed similarly robust NF-κB-dependent cytokine production following intraperitoneal LPS challenge. Human TNF levels in liver homogenates reached ~20 pg/mg tissue (~40× increase over PBS control; p<0.001, n~6-7 per group), IL-6 ~2.5 pg/mg (~80× increase; p<0.001), and IL-8 ~12 pg/mg (~12× increase; p<0.001) (Figure 2B). The magnitude of IL-6 induction in liver exceeded that observed in serum, suggesting that hepatic human myeloid cells may be particularly responsive to systemic inflammatory stimuli or that the liver serves as a major source of circulating IL-6 during endotoxemia (Bloomer and Moyer, 2021).

To assess whether human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice could respond to mucosal inflammatory challenges, we administered LPS intranasally and measured cytokine production in lung tissue. Intranasal LPS induced local production of human TNF (~100 pg/mg tissue, ~10× increase over PBS; p<0.001, n~6-7 per group), IL-6 (~6 pg/mg, ~60× increase; p<0.001), and IL-8 (~25 pg/mg, ~5× increase; p<0.001) in lung homogenates (Figure 2C). The robust cytokine responses in lung tissue demonstrate that human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice can mount localized inflammatory responses at mucosal surfaces, not only systemic responses to intraperitoneal challenges. These findings establish that humanized NSG-QUAD mice recapitulate NF-κB-dependent cytokine production in response to both systemic and mucosal LPS exposure.

Type I interferon pathway is activated in humanized NSG-QUAD mice

In addition to NF-κB activation, LPS can trigger type I interferon responses through TRIF-dependent signaling (Ivashkiv, 2003). To determine whether humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount type I interferon responses, we measured human interferon-α/β (IFNα/β) and the interferon-stimulated gene product CXCL10 (Huang et al., 2025) in serum and tissues following LPS challenge. Serum human IFNα/β levels did not change significantly after intraperitoneal LPS (p>0.05, n~6 per group) (Figure 2D), suggesting either that circulating IFNα/β levels remain below the detection threshold of the assay or that type I interferon production is compartmentalized to tissues. In contrast, human CXCL10 increased ~9-fold in serum (~90 pg/ml; p<0.001 vs. PBS ~10 pg/ml) following intraperitoneal LPS (Figure 2D), indicating functional type I interferon pathway activation.

Liver tissue showed a ~3.75-fold elevation in human CXCL10 (~300 pg/mg; p<0.001 vs. PBS ~80 pg/mg) after intraperitoneal LPS (Figure 2E), while human IFNα/β remained undetectable (p>0.05). The higher baseline CXCL10 levels in liver compared to serum may reflect constitutive expression by hepatic immune cells or stromal populations. Notably, lung tissue did not show significant CXCL10 induction after intranasal LPS (PBS ~400 vs. LPS ~600 pg/mg; p>0.05, n~6 per group) (Figure 2F), despite robust NF-κB cytokine responses in the same compartment. This compartment-specific pattern suggests that type I interferon pathway activation in humanized NSG-QUAD mice is more prominent in response to systemic LPS challenge than to mucosal exposure, possibly reflecting differences in local myeloid cell composition, TLR4 expression levels, or tissue-specific regulatory mechanisms (Stanifer et al., 2019).

NLRP3 inflammasome activation produces IL-1β and IL-18 in multiple compartments

The NLRP3 inflammasome is a multiprotein complex that responds to diverse danger signals by activating caspase-1, which cleaves pro-IL-1β and pro-IL-18 into their mature, secreted forms (Cullen et al., 2015) (Xu and Núñez, 2023). LPS provides both the priming signal (via NF-κB-dependent transcription of inflammasome components and pro-IL-1β) and, under certain conditions, the activation signal for NLRP3 inflammasome assembly. To determine whether human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice activate the NLRP3 inflammasome in response to LPS, we measured mature IL-1β and IL-18 in serum, bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) fluid, and tissue homogenates.

Serum human IL-1β increased ~12-fold after intraperitoneal LPS (PBS ~1 pg/ml vs. LPS ~12 pg/ml; p<0.001, n~6-7 per group), while IL-18 levels did not change significantly (p>0.05) (Figure 3A). The selective induction of IL-1β without corresponding IL-18 elevation is consistent with differential regulation of these two inflammasome substrates (Zehnder et al., 2003), as IL-1β requires both transcriptional upregulation and caspase-1 cleavage, whereas IL-18 is constitutively expressed in many cell types and may be subject to additional post-translational controls. In contrast to serum, BAL fluid showed a dramatic ~125-fold induction of human IL-1β after intranasal LPS (PBS ~0.02 pg/ml vs. LPS ~2.5 pg/ml; p<0.001, n~6-7 per group), with no significant IL-18 change (p>0.05) (Figure 3B). The markedly higher fold-induction of IL-1β in BAL compared to serum suggests that mucosal myeloid cells may be particularly competent for inflammasome activation, or that the local inflammatory milieu in lung tissue provides stronger activation signals.

Liver tissue demonstrated ~14-fold elevation of human IL-1β after intraperitoneal LPS (PBS ~1.0 pg/mg vs. LPS ~14 pg/mg; p<0.001, n~6-7 per group), while IL-18 remained unchanged (p>0.05) (Figure 3D). Notably, NF-κB-dependent cytokines (TNF, IL-6, IL-8) and the type I interferon-stimulated gene CXCL10 were robustly induced in all compartments examined (all p<0.001), confirming that the absence of IL-18 induction was not due to general unresponsiveness of human myeloid cells in these tissues. The coordinate induction of NF-κB cytokines, type I interferon markers, and IL-1β across multiple anatomical sites demonstrates that humanized NSG-QUAD mice recapitulate the full spectrum of innate immune signaling pathways activated by LPS.

To assess whether LPS-induced inflammasome activation was accompanied by myeloid cell recruitment to tissues, we quantified granulocyte and monocyte populations in spleen and liver after intraperitoneal LPS challenge. Splenic granulocyte counts increased ~6-fold with LPS (PBS ~30 vs. LPS ~190 cells/ml; p<0.001, n~6-7 per group), while monocyte subsets and dendritic cell counts showed no significant differences (all p>0.05) (Figure 3C). Liver granulocyte counts similarly increased after LPS (data not shown). The selective recruitment of granulocytes to spleen and liver suggests that LPS-induced chemokine production drives tissue infiltration of these cells, which may contribute to local cytokine production and inflammasome activation.

MCC950 specifically inhibits inflammasome-dependent cytokines without affecting NF-κB responses or monocyte viability

To validate that IL-1β production in humanized NSG-QUAD mice is mediated by the NLRP3 inflammasome and to assess the pharmacological tractability of the model, we pre-treated mice with MCC950, a specific small-molecule inhibitor of NLRP3 (Wu et al., 2020) [unverified]. Baseline characterization confirmed comparable human CD45+ engraftment (~40%, n~6-9 per group) and monocyte proportions (~13-14%) across PBS, LPS, and LPS+MCC950 groups prior to challenge (all p>0.05) (Figure 4A-D), ensuring that any differences in cytokine responses would reflect pharmacological inhibition rather than differences in human immune cell composition.

MCC950 pre-treatment (50 mg/kg intraperitoneally, 1 hour before LPS) reduced serum human IL-1β from ~180 pg/ml (LPS alone) to ~50 pg/ml (p<0.001 vs. LPS), approaching PBS control levels (~1 pg/ml) (Figure 4E). Serum IL-18 decreased from ~35 pg/ml (LPS) to ~10 pg/ml with MCC950 (p<0.05 vs. LPS), compared to PBS ~2 pg/ml (Figure 4F). The incomplete suppression of IL-18 compared to IL-1β may reflect the fact that IL-18 can be processed by caspases other than caspase-1 or that residual NLRP3 activity persists despite MCC950 treatment. Importantly, MCC950 did not affect LPS-induced production of NF-κB-dependent cytokines: serum TNF levels were ~150 ng/ml with LPS alone and ~60 ng/ml with LPS+MCC950 (p>0.05 vs. LPS) (Figure 4G), while IL-6 levels were ~18 ng/ml with LPS and ~12 ng/ml with LPS+MCC950 (p>0.05 vs. LPS) (Figure 4H). 

To determine whether MCC950 affects human monocyte viability, we quantified monocyte frequencies and absolute counts in peripheral blood after LPS challenge. Monocyte percentages decreased from ~13% (PBS) to ~5% (LPS; p<0.001), and absolute monocyte counts declined from ~6×10⁴ cells/ml (PBS) to ~2×10⁴ cells/ml (LPS; p<0.01) (Figure 4I-J). MCC950 did not rescue this LPS-induced monocyte decline (p>0.05 vs. LPS), indicating that the reduction in circulating monocytes reflects LPS-induced tissue sequestration or activation-induced cell death rather than NLRP3-dependent cytotoxicity. Analysis of dead monocyte populations showed that the percentage of dead monocytes increased from ~2% (PBS) to ~14% (LPS; p<0.001), with LPS+MCC950 showing ~15% dead monocytes (slightly higher than LPS alone, p<0.05) (Figure 4K). However, absolute counts of dead monocytes remained similar across all groups (~0.3-0.4×10⁴ cells/ml; p>0.05) (Figure 4L), indicating that the increased percentage of dead monocytes in LPS and LPS+MCC950 groups reflects the denominator effect of reduced total monocyte counts rather than increased absolute monocyte death.

# Discussion

The central finding of this study is that humanized NSG-QUAD mice mount coordinated, pharmacologically separable human innate immune responses in vivo—encompassing NF-κB-dependent cytokine production, type I interferon signaling, and NLRP3 inflammasome activation—across both systemic and mucosal compartments. This result directly addresses a longstanding gap in translational immunology by demonstrating that quadruple transgenic myeloid support is sufficient not only for human myeloid cell engraftment but for functional innate immune competence. The rapid engraftment kinetics observed at 6 weeks post-injection, combined with robust representation of monocytes, dendritic cells, and granulocytes across peripheral blood, spleen, and liver compartments (Figure 1), distinguish this model from conventional NSG mice, which show limited human myeloid development (Etchin et al., 2016). The presence of all three major monocyte subsets—classical, intermediate, and non-classical—in humanized NSG-QUAD mice (Figure 1D) suggests that the model recapitulates key aspects of human myeloid cell differentiation and homeostasis. These findings indicate that transgenic expression of human IL-3, GM-CSF, SCF, and CSF1 overcomes the myeloid developmental block characteristic of earlier humanized mouse platforms (Sefik et al., 2025).

Previous humanized mouse models have primarily focused on adaptive immunity, with T and B cell responses well-characterized but innate immunity poorly represented (Shultz et al., 2007). Recent efforts to improve myeloid engraftment through cytokine supplementation or transgenic expression have shown promise (Munteanu et al., 2025), yet functional validation of inflammasome responses has been limited (Sefik et al., 2022). Our findings extend prior work by demonstrating that human myeloid cells in NSG-QUAD mice not only engraft but also mount coordinated inflammatory responses involving multiple signaling pathways. The robust production of NF-κB-dependent cytokines (TNF, IL-6, IL-8) in response to both intraperitoneal and intranasal LPS challenges (Figure 2A–C) indicates that human myeloid cells in this model retain functional pattern recognition receptor signaling. The concurrent induction of CXCL10 (Figure 2D) demonstrates intact type I interferon pathway activation, while the substantial IL-1β and IL-18 secretion (Figure 3A–C) confirms functional NLRP3 inflammasome assembly and caspase-1 activation.

The differential cytokine responses between systemic and mucosal compartments suggest that tissue-specific factors influence human innate immune activation in this model. Intranasal LPS challenge elicited robust TNF, IL-6, and IL-8 production in lung tissue (Figure 2C) and IL-1β and IL-18 in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (Figure 3B), indicating that human myeloid cells can respond to mucosal inflammatory stimuli. In contrast, serum cytokine levels after intranasal challenge were lower than those observed after intraperitoneal LPS, suggesting compartmentalized inflammatory responses (Stojanovic et al., 2026). The robust IL-1β response with minimal IL-18 secretion in some compartments may reflect differential regulation of these inflammasome products, as IL-18 is constitutively expressed in some cell types and may not require de novo synthesis following inflammasome activation (Guo et al., 2023). The lack of serum IFNα2 elevation despite CXCL10 induction (Figure 2D) suggests that type I interferon signaling may be intact but IFNα production or stability may be limited in this model. It is possible that the kinetics of IFNα secretion differ from CXCL10 induction, or that human plasmacytoid dendritic cells—the primary IFNα-producing cells—are underrepresented in the reconstituted immune system. These findings suggest that CXCL10 induction in humanized NSG-QUAD mice may result from autocrine or paracrine type I interferon signaling (Sanda et al., 2006) rather than systemic IFNα release.

The pharmacological validation with MCC950 demonstrates that the model is suitable for testing innate immunity-targeted therapeutics. MCC950 selectively inhibited IL-1β and IL-18 production in both serum and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (Figure 4B) without affecting NF-κB-dependent cytokines TNF and IL-6 (Figure 4C), confirming that inflammasome activation in humanized NSG-QUAD mice follows the canonical two-signal model (Fontana and Vance, 2011). The first signal (LPS-induced NF-κB activation) primes inflammasome component expression and pro-IL-1β synthesis, while the second signal (NLRP3 activation) triggers inflammasome assembly and caspase-1-mediated cytokine maturation. The lack of MCC950 effect on monocyte viability (Figure 4D) indicates that the compound's anti-inflammatory effects are not due to cytotoxicity, consistent with its mechanism as a specific NLRP3 inhibitor that blocks inflammasome assembly without inducing cell death. These findings suggest that humanized NSG-QUAD mice can be used to evaluate the efficacy and selectivity of inflammasome-targeted therapeutics currently in clinical development (Coll et al., 2019).

The humanized NSG-QUAD model addresses a critical gap in translational immunology by enabling in vivo studies of human innate immune responses in a controlled experimental setting. Species differences between mice and humans in innate immune receptor repertoires, signaling pathway components, and cytokine biology have limited translation of preclinical findings from conventional mouse models (Zschaler et al., 2014). The ability to study human myeloid cells responding to inflammatory stimuli in vivo may be particularly valuable for diseases where these species differences are most pronounced. This model could be used to study human-specific aspects of inflammasome biology, including genetic variants in NLRP3, PYCARD, and CASP1 associated with autoinflammatory diseases (Nurmi et al., 2024). The pharmacological tractability of the model, demonstrated by the selective MCC950 effect, makes it suitable for preclinical testing of innate immunity-targeted therapeutics, potentially improving translation to human clinical trials (Deng et al., 2021). The ability to test human-specific therapeutics—including monoclonal antibodies targeting human cytokines or cell surface receptors—in this model could accelerate drug development for inflammasome-driven diseases (Seo et al., 2025).

The model has several limitations that should be considered when interpreting results. The absence of human stromal cells and tissue-resident immune cells may affect the magnitude and kinetics of inflammatory responses compared to humans (Roan et al., 2019) (Gray and Farber, 2022). Mouse endothelial cells, fibroblasts, and epithelial cells may not provide optimal cytokine and chemokine signals for human myeloid cell recruitment and activation, potentially dampening or altering inflammatory responses. The use of cord blood-derived HSPCs may introduce donor-to-donor variability in engraftment and functional responses, as cord blood contains a heterogeneous population of stem and progenitor cells with variable differentiation potential. The 6-week time point captures early myeloid engraftment but may not reflect steady-state human immune cell composition; longer reconstitution periods might yield different myeloid subset distributions or functional properties. The LPS challenge model, while useful for validating innate immune function, represents an acute inflammatory stimulus that may not fully recapitulate chronic inflammatory diseases characterized by sustained low-grade inflammation or repeated inflammatory insults. We cannot fully exclude the possibility that residual mouse immune cells contribute to some of the observed responses, though the use of human-specific cytokine measurements and the demonstration of MCC950 sensitivity argue against significant mouse cell involvement. the lack of human adaptive immune cells at this early time point means that innate-adaptive immune crosstalk—which may be important in many inflammatory diseases—is not captured in this model.

The humanized NSG-QUAD model provides a platform for mechanistic studies of human innate immunity that are not feasible in conventional mouse models or human in vitro systems. Unlike in vitro monocyte or macrophage cultures, this model allows study of human myeloid cells in a three-dimensional tissue environment with physiological cell-cell interactions and systemic inflammatory mediator distribution. Unlike conventional mouse models, it enables direct study of human-specific innate immune mechanisms without the confounding effects of species differences in receptor expression, signaling pathway components, or cytokine biology. Future studies could extend this model to chronic inflammatory conditions by repeated or sustained inflammatory challenges, infection models using human-tropic pathogens, or combination with human tissue grafts to increase physiological relevance. Incorporating human tissue organoids or engineered tissue constructs could provide more physiologically relevant microenvironments for studying tissue-specific inflammatory responses while maintaining the advantages of in vivo experimentation.

Future work should characterize additional innate immune pathways in this model, including other pattern recognition receptors such as TLR4, TLR7/8, and cytosolic DNA sensors, as well as other inflammasomes including NLRC4, AIM2, and pyrin. Longitudinal studies examining immune cell development and function beyond 6 weeks would clarify the stability and maturation of the human immune system in NSG-QUAD mice, including whether myeloid cell subset distributions shift over time and whether functional responses remain consistent. Testing additional inflammasome inhibitors and other innate immunity-targeted drugs—including IL-1 receptor antagonists, IL-18 binding protein, and caspase-1 inhibitors—would further validate the model's utility for preclinical pharmacology and enable comparative efficacy studies. Combining humanized NSG-QUAD mice with disease-relevant genetic modifications, either in the mouse host or in the human HSPCs, could create models of specific autoinflammatory or inflammatory diseases for mechanistic study and therapeutic testing.

# Declarations

**Data Availability.** [FILL: accession numbers / repository]

**Competing Interests.** The authors declare no competing interests.

**Ethics Statement.** All procedures were approved by the Institutional Animal Ethics Committee.


# References

1. Mestas J, Hughes CC. Of mice and not men: differences between mouse and human immunology. J Immunol. 2004;172(5):2731-8. https://doi.org/10.4049/jimmunol.172.5.2731

2. Brehm MA, Kenney LL, Wiles MV, Low BE, Tisch RM, Burzenski L, Mueller C, Greiner DL, Shultz LD. Lack of acute xenogeneic graft- versus-host disease, but retention of T-cell function following engraftment of human peripheral blood mononuclear cells in NSG mice deficient in MHC class I and II expression. FASEB J. 2019;33(3):3137-3151. https://doi.org/10.1096/fj.201800636R

3. Kong D, Ryu JC, Shin N, Lee SE, Kim NG, Kim HY, Kim MJ, Choi J, Kim DH, Kang KS. In Vitro Modeling of Atherosclerosis Using iPSC-Derived Blood Vessel Organoids. Adv Healthc Mater. 2025;14(1):e2400919. https://doi.org/10.1002/adhm.202400919

4. Lu YC, Yeh WC, Ohashi PS. LPS/TLR4 signal transduction pathway. Cytokine. 2008;42(2):145-151. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cyto.2008.01.006

5. Mukherjee T, Kumar N, Chawla M, Philpott DJ, Basak S. The NF-κB signaling system in the immunopathogenesis of inflammatory bowel disease. Sci Signal. 2024;17(818):eadh1641. https://doi.org/10.1126/scisignal.adh1641

6. Rice GI, Melki I, Frémond ML, Briggs TA, Rodero MP, Kitabayashi N, Oojageer A, Bader-Meunier B, Belot A, Bodemer C, Quartier P, Crow YJ. Assessment of Type I Interferon Signaling in Pediatric Inflammatory Disease. J Clin Immunol. 2017;37(2):123-132. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10875-016-0359-1

7. Shultz LD, Ishikawa F, Greiner DL. Humanized mice in translational biomedical research. Nat Rev Immunol. 2007;7(2):118-30. https://doi.org/10.1038/nri2017

8. Stocks H, De Leeuw E, Lambrecht BN, Vandekerckhove L, van Loo G, Wullaert A. Development of human innate immune responses in a humanized mouse model expressing four human myelopoiesis transgenes. Front Immunol. 2024;15:1419117. https://doi.org/10.3389/fimmu.2024.1419117

9. Wouters F, van der Hilst J, Bogie J. Lipids in inflammasome activation and autoinflammatory disorders. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2024;153(1):1-11. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaci.2023.10.008

10. Zipfel C. Plant pattern-recognition receptors. Trends Immunol. 2014;35(7):345-51. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.it.2014.05.004

11. Li H, Guan Y, Liang B, Ding P, Hou X, Wei W, Ma Y. Therapeutic potential of MCC950, a specific inhibitor of NLRP3 inflammasome. Eur J Pharmacol. 2022;928:175091. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejphar.2022.175091

12. Bloomer SA, Moyer ED. Hepatic macrophage accumulation with aging: cause for concern?. Am J Physiol Gastrointest Liver Physiol. 2021;320(4):G496-G505. https://doi.org/10.1152/ajpgi.00286.2020

13. Crespo J, Vatan L, Maj T, Liu R, Kryczek I, Zou W. Phenotype and tissue distribution of CD28H+ immune cell subsets. Oncoimmunology. 2017;6(12):e1362529. https://doi.org/10.1080/2162402X.2017.1362529

14. Cullen SP, Kearney CJ, Clancy DM, Martin SJ. Diverse Activators of the NLRP3 Inflammasome Promote IL-1β Secretion by Triggering Necrosis. Cell Rep. 2015;11(10):1535-48. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.celrep.2015.05.003

15. Fu YJ, Xu B, Huang SW, Luo X, Deng XL, Luo S, Liu C, Wang Q, Chen JY, Zhou L. Baicalin prevents LPS-induced activation of TLR4/NF-κB p65 pathway and inflammation in mice via inhibiting the expression of CD14. Acta Pharmacol Sin. 2021;42(1):88-96. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41401-020-0411-9

16. Huang CH, Laurent-Rolle M, Grove TL, Hsu JC. Interferon-Stimulated Genes and Immune Metabolites as Broad-Spectrum Biomarkers for Viral Infections. Viruses. 2025;17(1). https://doi.org/10.3390/v17010132

17. Ivashkiv LB. Type I interferon modulation of cellular responses to cytokines and infectious pathogens: potential role in SLE pathogenesis. Autoimmunity. 2003;36(8):473-9. https://doi.org/10.1080/08916930310001605882

18. Kapellos TS, Bonaguro L, Gemünd I, Reusch N, Saglam A, Hinkley ER, Schultze JL. Human Monocyte Subsets and Phenotypes in Major Chronic Inflammatory Diseases. Front Immunol. 2019;10:2035. https://doi.org/10.3389/fimmu.2019.02035

19. Nam NH. Naturally occurring NF-kappaB inhibitors. Mini Rev Med Chem. 2006;6(8):945-51. https://doi.org/10.2174/138955706777934937

20. Stanifer ML, Pervolaraki K, Boulant S. Differential Regulation of Type I and Type III Interferon Signaling. Int J Mol Sci. 2019;20(6). https://doi.org/10.3390/ijms20061445

21. Wu D, Chen Y, Sun Y, Gao Q, Li H, Yang Z, Wang Y, Jiang X, Yu B. Target of MCC950 in Inhibition of NLRP3 Inflammasome Activation: a Literature Review. Inflammation. 2020;43(1):17-23. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10753-019-01098-8

22. Xu J, Núñez G. The NLRP3 inflammasome: activation and regulation. Trends Biochem Sci. 2023;48(4):331-344. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tibs.2022.10.002

23. Zehnder M, Delaleu N, Du Y, Bickel M. Cytokine gene expression--part of host defence in pulpitis. Cytokine. 2003;22(3-4):84-8. https://doi.org/10.1016/s1043-4666(03)00116-9

24. Coll RC, Hill JR, Day CJ, Zamoshnikova A, Boucher D, Massey NL, Chitty JL, Fraser JA, Jennings MP, Robertson AAB, Schroder K. MCC950 directly targets the NLRP3 ATP-hydrolysis motif for inflammasome inhibition. Nat Chem Biol. 2019;15(6):556-559. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41589-019-0277-7

25. Deng Y, Xie M, Li Q, Xu X, Ou W, Zhang Y, Xiao H, Yu H, Zheng Y, Liang Y, Jiang C, Chen G, Du D, Zheng W, Wang S, Gong M, Chen Y, Tian R, Li T. Targeting Mitochondria-Inflammation Circuit by β-Hydroxybutyrate Mitigates HFpEF. Circ Res. 2021;128(2):232-245. https://doi.org/10.1161/CIRCRESAHA.120.317933

26. Etchin J, Montero J, Berezovskaya A, Le BT, Kentsis A, Christie AL, Conway AS, Chen WC, Reed C, Mansour MR, Ng CE, Adamia S, Rodig SJ, Galinsky IA, Stone RM, Klebanov B, Landesman Y, Kauffman M, Shacham S, Kung AL, Wang JC, Letai A, Look AT. Activity of a selective inhibitor of nuclear export, selinexor (KPT-330), against AML-initiating cells engrafted into immunosuppressed NSG mice. Leukemia. 2016;30(1):190-9. https://doi.org/10.1038/leu.2015.194

27. Fontana MF, Vance RE. Two signal models in innate immunity. Immunol Rev. 2011;243(1):26-39. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1600-065X.2011.01037.x

28. Gray JI, Farber DL. Tissue-Resident Immune Cells in Humans. Annu Rev Immunol. 2022;40:195-220. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-immunol-093019-112809

29. Guo Y, Zhang H, Lv Z, Du Y, Li D, Fang H, You J, Yu L, Li R. Up-regulated CD38 by daphnetin alleviates lipopolysaccharide-induced lung injury via inhibiting MAPK/NF-κB/NLRP3 pathway. Cell Commun Signal. 2023;21(1):66. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12964-023-01041-3

30. Munteanu R, Gulei D, Moldovan CS, Azzoni E, Belver L, Feder R, Pirv S, Buzoianu AD, Einsele H, Mittelman M, Ghiaur G, Hasserjian R, Tomuleasa C. Humanized mouse models in MDS. Cell Death Dis. 2025;16(1):531. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41419-025-07861-0

31. Nurmi K, Silventoinen K, Keskitalo S, Rajamäki K, Kouri VP, Kinnunen M, Jalil S, Maldonado R, Wartiovaara K, Nievas EI, Denita-Juárez SP, Duncan CJA, Kuismin O, Saarela J, Romo I, Martelius T, Parantainen J, Beklen A, Bilicka M, Matikainen S, Nordström DC, Kaustio M, Wartiovaara-Kautto U, Kilpivaara O, Klein C, Hauck F, Jahkola T, Hautala T, Varjosalo M, Barreto G, Seppänen MRJ, Eklund KK. Truncating NFKB1 variants cause combined NLRP3 inflammasome activation and type I interferon signaling and predispose to necrotizing fasciitis. Cell Rep Med. 2024;5(4):101503. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.xcrm.2024.101503

32. Roan F, Obata-Ninomiya K, Ziegler SF. Epithelial cell-derived cytokines: more than just signaling the alarm. J Clin Invest. 2019;129(4):1441-1451. https://doi.org/10.1172/JCI124606

33. Sanda C, Weitzel P, Tsukahara T, Schaley J, Edenberg HJ, Stephens MA, McClintick JN, Blatt LM, Li L, Brodsky L, Taylor MW. Differential gene induction by type I and type II interferons and their combination. J Interferon Cytokine Res. 2006;26(7):462-72. https://doi.org/10.1089/jir.2006.26.462

34. Sefik E, Qu R, Junqueira C, Kaffe E, Mirza H, Zhao J, Brewer JR, Han A, Steach HR, Israelow B, Blackburn HN, Velazquez SE, Chen YG, Halene S, Iwasaki A, Meffre E, Nussenzweig M, Lieberman J, Wilen CB, Kluger Y, Flavell RA. Inflammasome activation in infected macrophages drives COVID-19 pathology. Nature. 2022;606(7914):585-593. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-022-04802-1

35. Sefik E, Philbrick W, Zhang F, Agrawal K, Van Lee B, Sam J, Karatepe K, Zheng Y, Liang K, Peng S, Mirza H, Rangavajhula A, Simon P, Arun N, Babu P, Eynon E, Chiorazzi M, Shan L, Halene S, Luo HR, Rongvaux A, Kluger Y, Flavell RA. Humanization of CD47 enables development of functional human neutrophils via postirradiation remodeling of the bone marrow. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A. 2025;122(38):e2426546122. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2426546122

36. Seo W, Jung B, Roh T, Shin HJ, Song IC, Chung C, Jo EK. Inflammasomes and pyroptosis in cancer: mechanisms and therapeutic advances. J Hematol Oncol. 2025;18(1):113. https://doi.org/10.1186/s13045-025-01763-6

37. Stojanovic A, Giorgetta S, Vonhören D, Krieghoff-Henning EI, Platten M, Loges S, Tugues S, Cerwenka A. Tissue-Specific Spatial Regulation of Innate Immune Checkpoints in Cancer. Cancer Discov. 2026;16(5):857-875. https://doi.org/10.1158/2159-8290.CD-25-2112

38. Zschaler J, Schlorke D, Arnhold J. Differences in innate immune response between man and mouse. Crit Rev Immunol. 2014;34(5):433-54.
